nativeRange.habitat: Riverbanks and shaded stream margins in West African tropical rainforest; grows epiphytically attached to wood, roots, and rocks at the water margin, often emersed in nature

decorPreferences: shaded areas to reduce leaf algae

goodWith: African cichlids (tough leaves resist most nibbling)

summary: The Coffee Leaf Anubias (Anubias barteri var. coffeefolia) is a slow-growing cultivar prized for its deeply corrugated leaves, which emerge with a distinctive bronze-coffee tint before maturing to glossy dark green. It is widely considered one of the easiest aquarium plants to keep, provided the rhizome is never buried in the substrate.
careNotes:
The Coffee Leaf Anubias is a cultivar of Anubias barteri, distinguished from the standard form by its rippled, corrugated leaf surface and the striking bronze-coffee coloration of newly emerging leaves, which mature to a deep glossy green over time. Like all Anubias, it is a slow-growing epiphytic rhizome plant — meaning it anchors itself to surfaces rather than rooting into substrate — and it thrives under low to medium light without requiring CO2 injection, though CO2 supplementation is reported to improve leaf quality and modestly accelerate growth.

CRITICAL — DO NOT BURY THE RHIZOME. The rhizome is the thick horizontal stem from which leaves and roots emerge. Burying it in substrate causes it to rot, leading to rapid plant decline and death. Attach the plant to driftwood, rocks, or other hardscape using thread, fishing line, or cyanoacrylate (super) glue, or wedge it securely into a crevice. Roots will anchor on their own over time.

Adult height is most commonly reported at 10–15 cm (4–6 in), with well-established specimens reaching up to approximately 25 cm (10 in). This makes the Coffeefolia a medium-sized cultivar — noticeably smaller than the broad-leaf parent form, which typically reaches 25–45 cm, but larger than compact cultivars such as 'Nana Petite'. Midground placement tends to show off the ruffled leaves and new-growth coloration most effectively.

Low-light conditions are preferred and generally produce the best leaf coloration while limiting algae growth on the slow-growing leaves. Brighter lighting can speed leaf production but tends to increase algae accumulation on the leaf surface. Positioning the plant in shaded areas of the aquarium — beneath taller plants or behind hardscape — is a reliable way to keep leaves algae-free. Liquid fertilization is beneficial; because nutrient uptake occurs primarily through water-column roots, substrate quality has little effect on plant health. CO2 is not required but is reported to improve growth rate and leaf quality.
breedingNotes: Propagated by rhizome division: using a sharp, clean blade, cut a healthy rhizome into sections, each retaining at least three to four healthy leaves and some attached roots. Re-attach each section to hardscape and do not bury the cut end. As with other Anubias cultivars, underwater flowering is occasionally observed — the plant produces a small, pale inflorescence (a hooded spathe enclosing a central spike called a spadix, the characteristic flower structure of the Araceae family) — but seed propagation is not practical in a home aquarium setting.

primary.name: Modern Aquarium
primary.notes: Primary source page for Anubias barteri var. coffeefolia. Confirmed origin (West Africa), care level (easy), light demand (low to moderate), CO2 (not required), growth rate (slow), midground placement, epiphyte/rhizome handling, temperature tolerance 65–86 °F (18–30 °C), and rippled/corrugated leaf morphology.

additional.name: Buce Plant
additional.notes: Cross-checked family (Araceae), origin (West Africa), care level (easy), pH (6.0–7.5), light (low), CO2 (not necessary but accelerates growth), growth rate (slow to moderate), adult size (4–6 inches typical, up to ~12 inches / ~30 cm), midground-to-background placement, propagation (rhizome division), and the coffee-colored new-growth maturing to dark green. Buce Plant also explicitly warns that burying the rhizome causes melt and death.

additional.name: Anubias barteri (fw-plant-004) – AquaMate sister entry
additional.notes: Cultivar-of-parent reference. Coffeefolia is a cultivar of A. barteri and shares the same fundamental care profile (epiphytic habit, rhizome handling, lighting/CO2 demand, parameter tolerances). Used to confirm temperature and gH ranges where coffeefolia-specific sources were silent.
